_metadata.description: Experiential learning — the brain-aligned sequencing principle (Experience → Observation → Discussion → Explanation → Theory), multi-region neural activation, learning-precedes-language, and educator-as-designer shift. Audit indicators for experience-first vs explanation-first sequencing.
_metadata.runtime_strategy_rationale: Body MD ~10,203 chars / 4 ≈ 2,551 tokens — within the 'either' band per runtime_strategy_rules. Set to read_at_runtime per named_override_patterns.selective_load: canvas_course_expert loads 2-3 of ~12 knowledge files per audit task, not all as a stable prefix. Selective-load defeats embed-cache benefits.

scope: Brain-aligned sequencing principle for course delivery — experience before explanation, multi-region neural activation, learning-precedes-language. Supplies the neuroscience rationale for why content is not the destination. Boundaries: does not cover Hattie phase progression (see hattie_3phase) or working-memory mechanics (see cognitive_load_theory).

facts.definition: Traditional higher education sequences instruction as explanation first, experience second. Neuroscience research consistently shows the opposite: the brain forms durable neural pathways when learners interact with problems, experiment, receive feedback, and reflect — and then receive formal explanation. 'When experience comes first, explanation sticks.'

Experience → Observation → Discussion → Explanation → Theory. The operative inversion of the traditional Theory → Worked example → Practice → Apply sequence. Activates sensory, motor, decision-making, and emotional regions rather than only language and short-term memory.

Aswad identifies the transfer gap as instructional design failure, not student failure. Students may 'know' a formula yet struggle to apply it in unfamiliar contexts — this is structural, lives in the sequence of delivery, not in the learner. Explanation-first produces surface learning that fails the transfer test (Hattie Phase 3).

Humans grasp cause-effect, risk, patterns and systems long before they can formally articulate them. Explanation and terminology are most effective when they arrive AFTER learners have encountered a concept in action. Design implication: module overviews, vocabulary lists, and frameworks should follow — not precede — encounter with the phenomenon. The dominant LMS pattern (overview → reading → quiz) inverts this.

Lecture/reading activates language and short-term memory. Experiential learning activates sensory processing, motor control, decision-making, and emotional engagement. Each additional region engaged is another retrieval cue. Extends CLT's dual-channel theory: dual channels don't compete; multi-region activation strengthens schema formation across more pathways.

Programming: make systems work, debug, observe outcomes → then name constructs (loops, variables, classes). Cybersecurity: experience simulated breaches → then study formal threat models. AI: train simple models, observe bias/data dependency → then study ML theory. Data Warehousing/SQL: build pipeline against real data, observe what breaks → then introduce normalization and dimensional modeling. Unifying principle: competence emerges from interaction, not exposition.

Educators move from primary transmitters of information to designers of learning experiences: (1) structuring meaningful challenges, (2) curating environments for safe experimentation, (3) guiding reflection and conceptualisation, (4) connecting experience to disciplinary frameworks. Same shift Designer Thinking describes — content as tool, not destination — with the neural rationale attached.

Add a sequencing field to relevant audit issues: experience_first (module/item delivers experience before explanation — pass); explanation_first (delivers explanation before any experience — flag); not_applicable (e.g., reference page). Pair with hattie_phase, cognitive_load_type, and learning_domain for a four-axis diagnosis.

Flag: module starts with long reading/lecture before any activity; vocabulary/framework introduced before encounter with phenomenon; first assignment is terminology quiz. Pass: module opens with problem/simulation/build, theory follows; theory positioned as 'how to explain what you just observed'.
citations: This knowledge file's Canvas Audit Indicators section

facts.definition: Flag: module is text-only (read → quiz → next). Pass: module pairs reading with hands-on build/discussion/artifact creation; reflection prompts connect experience to concept; activities require decision-making under uncertainty.

Flag (transmission mode): instructions read as 'follow these steps to get the right answer'; rubrics reward correct procedure execution. Pass (designer mode): instructions read as 'here is the situation, here are constraints, design a response'; rubrics reward sound judgment under ambiguity.

sources: Aswad, M. How does the brain learn? And why don't we teach that way? Times Higher Education, Campus (2024). Aswad is academic vice-principal at the Nasser Centre for Science and Technology.

pre_run_checklist: Each entry in consumed_by[] resolves to an existing agent spec
post_run_checklist: knowledge/experiential_learning_knowledge.json exists with shape=reference and facts[] present

changes: Retrofit: JSON companion authored for pre-existing canvas-toolbox knowledge MD.
MD body and canvas-toolbox header convention preserved.
Runtime strategy set to read_at_runtime via selective_load override (either band; selective audit-task loading pattern).
